flashcards.front: When do we say that work is done?
flashcards.back: Work is done when a force is applied on an object and the object is displaced in the direction of the force.

flashcards.front: Write the formula for work done.
flashcards.back: W = F × s, where F is force and s is displacement in the direction of force.

flashcards.front: Define 1 joule of work.
flashcards.back: Work done when a force of 1 N displaces an object by 1 m in the direction of the force.

flashcards.front: What is energy?
flashcards.back: Energy is the capacity to do work.

flashcards.front: What is kinetic energy?
flashcards.back: The energy possessed by an object due to its motion.

flashcards.front: Write the formula for kinetic energy.
flashcards.back: Ek = ½ mv², where m is mass and v is velocity.

flashcards.front: What is potential energy?
flashcards.back: The energy possessed by an object due to its position or configuration.

flashcards.front: Write the formula for gravitational potential energy.
flashcards.back: Ep = mgh, where m is mass, g is acceleration due to gravity, and h is height.

flashcards.front: State the law of conservation of energy.
flashcards.back: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only change from one form to another, and the total energy remains constant.

flashcards.front: What is mechanical energy?
flashcards.back: The sum of kinetic energy and potential energy of an object.

flashcards.front: Define power.
flashcards.back: Power is the rate of doing work or the rate of transfer of energy.

flashcards.front: Write the formula for power.
flashcards.back: P = W/t, where W is work done and t is time taken.

flashcards.front: What is the SI unit of power?
flashcards.back: Watt (W), defined as 1 joule of work done per second.

flashcards.front: What is 1 kilowatt?
Why is work not done when you push a wall?
flashcards.back: Because there is no displacement of the wall despite the force applied.

flashcards.front: Why is work done considered positive when lifting an object?
flashcards.back: Because the force applied is in the same direction as the displacement.

flashcards.front: Why is work done considered negative when friction acts on a moving object?
flashcards.back: Because friction acts opposite to the direction of displacement.

flashcards.front: Why does a moving bullet pierce a target?
flashcards.back: Because it possesses kinetic energy due to its motion.

flashcards.front: Why does a stretched rubber band have energy?
flashcards.back: Because work done on it is stored as potential energy.

flashcards.front: Why does a raised hammer drive a nail into wood?
flashcards.back: Because it possesses gravitational potential energy which converts into kinetic energy when released.

flashcards.front: Why do athletes land on sand pits during long jump?
flashcards.back: To increase the time of impact, reducing force by lowering the rate of change of momentum.

flashcards.front: Why is a powerful motorbike faster than a less powerful one?
flashcards.back: Because it can transfer energy at a higher rate, i.e., it has greater power.
